stimulus: The city council recently proposed banning the use of plastic bags at grocery stores, arguing that such a ban would significantly reduce plastic waste in the local landfill. However, studies show that when plastic bag bans are implemented, most shoppers switch to reusable canvas bags. Manufacturing a single canvas bag requires twenty times more energy than manufacturing a single plastic bag, and a canvas bag must be used at least 131 times before its environmental impact is lower than that of the plastic bags it replaces. Surveys indicate that the average shopper replaces a canvas bag after only 52 uses.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the city council's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some grocery stores in the city already offer paper bags as an alternative to plastic bags.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Residents who use canvas bags tend to be more environmentally conscious in other areas of their daily lives as well.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Plastic bags discarded in the landfill take hundreds of years to decompose, releasing harmful chemicals into the soil.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The energy required to produce canvas bags results in greenhouse gas emissions that contribute more to environmental harm than the plastic bags the canvas bags replace.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The city council has successfully implemented other environmental regulations that reduced waste in the local landfill.

stimulus: All of the paintings displayed in the Hartwell Gallery's permanent collection were created before 1900. Some of the paintings displayed in the Hartwell Gallery's permanent collection were influenced by Japanese woodblock prints. Therefore, some paintings influenced by Japanese woodblock prints were created before 1900.
stem: The argument's conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All paintings created before 1900 were influenced by Japanese woodblock prints.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some paintings influenced by Japanese woodblock prints are displayed in the Hartwell Gallery's permanent collection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No paintings created after 1900 were influenced by Japanese woodblock prints.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All paintings influenced by Japanese woodblock prints are displayed in the Hartwell Gallery's permanent collection.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some paintings displayed in the Hartwell Gallery's permanent collection were not influenced by Japanese woodblock prints.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long recommended that people reduce their sodium intake to lower blood pressure. A recent study, however, found that participants who reduced their sodium intake by 40 percent over six months showed no statistically significant reduction in blood pressure compared to a control group that made no dietary changes. Therefore, reducing sodium intake does not lower blood pressure.
stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: relies on a study whose sample size was not specified in the argument

choices.label: B
choices.text: fails to consider that blood pressure may be influenced by factors other than sodium intake

choices.label: C
choices.text: treats evidence that a single reduction amount had no measurable effect as proof that no reduction amount could have any effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: contradicts established nutritional science without providing sufficient expert testimony

choices.label: E
choices.text: assumes that the control group's blood pressure remained constant throughout the study period

stimulus: The city of Harwick recently passed an ordinance requiring all restaurants to post calorie counts on their menus. The city council argued that this measure would reduce obesity rates by allowing customers to make more informed dietary choices. However, a study conducted two years after the ordinance took effect found that average caloric intake among Harwick residents who dine out had not decreased. Therefore, the ordinance has failed to achieve its intended goal.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Several restaurants in neighboring cities voluntarily began posting calorie counts around the same time Harwick passed its ordinance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obesity is influenced by factors other than caloric intake, including genetics and physical activity levels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Before the ordinance, Harwick residents who dined out consumed significantly more calories than the national average, but they now consume calories at a rate consistent with the national average.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The study measured caloric intake only among residents who dine out at least three times per week.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Many Harwick restaurant owners complained that posting calorie counts was costly and administratively burdensome.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long recommended that people consume less sodium in order to reduce their risk of high blood pressure. A recent study, however, found that participants who reduced their sodium intake by 40 percent showed no significant decrease in blood pressure compared to a control group that made no dietary changes. Therefore, reducing sodium intake does not reduce the risk of high blood pressure.
stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: relies on a study whose participants may not be representative of the broader population

choices.label: B
choices.text: fails to consider that nutritionists may have motives other than improving public health

choices.label: C
choices.text: assumes that a decrease in blood pressure would occur immediately rather than over a long period of time

choices.label: D
choices.text: overlooks the possibility that factors other than sodium intake also contribute to high blood pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: draws a conclusion about risk reduction from evidence concerning only blood pressure levels

stimulus: A recent study found that cities with more coffee shops per capita have higher average rates of reported anxiety disorders than cities with fewer coffee shops per capita. The study's authors concluded that coffee consumption is a leading cause of anxiety disorders in urban populations.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion drawn by the study's authors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some people who suffer from anxiety disorders report that drinking coffee temporarily relieves their symptoms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cities with higher populations tend to have both more coffee shops per capita and better mental health reporting infrastructure, leading to higher rates of diagnosed anxiety disorders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The coffee shops counted in the study included establishments that also served alcohol and other beverages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anxiety disorders are more commonly diagnosed in adults between the ages of 25 and 44, who also tend to consume more coffee than other age groups.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several cities with a high number of coffee shops per capita also have above-average rates of unemployment, which is known to contribute to stress.

stimulus: The city of Harwick recently replaced its traditional sodium streetlights with LED streetlights in order to reduce energy costs. City officials claim the project was a financial success because the new LED lights consume 60 percent less electricity than the sodium lights did. However, the project cannot be considered a true financial success, because the city's total annual expenditure on street lighting has not decreased since the LED lights were installed.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the apparent discrepancy in the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: LED streetlights have a longer operational lifespan than sodium streetlights and therefore require replacement less frequently.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The city of Harwick increased the number of streetlights throughout the city by nearly 150 percent as part of the same infrastructure project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some residents complained that the sodium streetlights produced a warmer, more aesthetically pleasing light than the LED streetlights do.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cost of electricity in Harwick has remained relatively stable over the past several years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: City officials in neighboring towns have reported similar reductions in per-unit energy consumption after switching to LED streetlights.

stimulus: The city council recently voted to extend the downtown library's operating hours from 8 hours per day to 12 hours per day. The council cited increased community access as the primary justification. However, library attendance figures show that 90 percent of all library visits occur during the original 8-hour window. Furthermore, the extended hours will require hiring additional staff, significantly increasing the library's annual operating budget.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the council's justification for extending the library's operating hours?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Several neighboring cities have recently extended the hours of their own public libraries without increasing staffing costs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The 10 percent of visits that occur outside the original hours are made almost exclusively by working adults who have no other available time to visit the library.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Community surveys indicate that most residents are unaware that the library's hours have been extended.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The additional staff hired for the extended hours would primarily be part-time employees earning lower wages than current full-time staff.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The residents who visit during the original 8-hour window tend to borrow more materials per visit than those who visit during the extended hours.

stimulus: The city council argues that building a new downtown parking garage will reduce traffic congestion because more drivers will park downtown rather than circling the streets looking for open spots. However, studies of urban areas consistently show that increasing the supply of parking in a city center causes the total number of people who drive into that area to increase significantly, ultimately resulting in more vehicle traffic than existed before the new parking was built.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the city council's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The proposed parking garage would be financed through a combination of municipal bonds and private investment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some drivers who currently avoid downtown due to parking difficulties would begin driving downtown if additional parking were available.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The city council has successfully reduced congestion in other parts of the city through infrastructure improvements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A portion of the people who would use the new garage currently travel downtown by public transit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Traffic congestion downtown is most severe during morning and evening rush hours rather than on weekends.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long recommended that people consume fewer processed foods and more whole foods in order to maintain good health. A recent study, however, found that participants who strictly followed a whole-food diet for six months showed no significant improvement in key health markers compared to a control group that made no dietary changes. Therefore, the nutritionists' recommendation is misguided.
stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: assumes without justification that the control group was comparable in all relevant respects to the group following the whole-food diet

choices.label: B
choices.text: relies on the testimony of nutritionists, whose professional interests may bias their dietary recommendations

choices.label: C
choices.text: draws a broad conclusion about the value of a dietary recommendation based on a study that measured only short-term outcomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: mistakes a correlation between whole-food consumption and health for a claim about causation

choices.label: E
choices.text: fails to consider whether the participants in the study accurately reported their adherence to the prescribed diet

stimulus: The city council recently proposed a mandatory recycling program, arguing that it would significantly reduce the amount of waste sent to the local landfill. However, studies of similar programs in other cities show that mandatory recycling increases the total volume of materials collected but does not proportionally reduce landfill waste, because residents who recycle tend to feel licensed to consume more disposable goods overall. Therefore, the council's recycling program will not achieve its intended goal.
stem: The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Residents in the city will respond to the mandatory recycling program in the same way that residents in other cities responded to similar programs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The local landfill is currently operating near its maximum capacity and cannot accommodate additional waste.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mandatory recycling programs are more expensive to administer than voluntary recycling programs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The city council has not considered any waste-reduction strategies other than mandatory recycling.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Residents who do not recycle consume fewer disposable goods than residents who do recycle.

stimulus: The city council argues that building a new downtown stadium will boost the local economy because stadiums attract tourists, and tourists spend money at local businesses. However, studies of cities that have built stadiums in the past two decades show that, in most cases, total consumer spending in those cities did not increase after stadium construction. Economists explain this by noting that residents who attend stadium events simply redirect money they would have spent at restaurants, movie theaters, and other local venues.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the economists' explanation for why stadium construction fails to increase total consumer spending?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some cities that built stadiums did experience modest increases in total consumer spending in the years following construction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tourists who visit a city specifically to attend stadium events also spend money at hotels, restaurants, and shops that local residents do not typically patronize.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The cost of constructing a downtown stadium is typically borne partly by local taxpayers through municipal bonds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Residents who attend stadium events report that they find such events more enjoyable than the activities they previously spent money on.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several cities have rejected stadium proposals after reviewing the economic studies mentioned by the economists.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long recommended that people consume less sodium in order to reduce the risk of high blood pressure. However, a recent large-scale study found that participants who reduced their sodium intake showed no statistically significant decrease in blood pressure compared to those who maintained their normal sodium intake. Therefore, the nutritionists' recommendation is misguided.
stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it fails to consider which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether high blood pressure has causes other than sodium intake that were not controlled for in the study

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the participants in the study accurately reported their sodium consumption throughout the study period

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether blood pressure is the only health outcome that could be affected by reducing sodium intake

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether reducing sodium intake, even if it does not lower blood pressure, might still reduce the risk of high blood pressure developing in people who do not yet have it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the nutritionists who made the recommendation had reviewed prior studies on sodium and blood pressure

stimulus: The city council recently voted to require all new residential buildings to include a minimum number of green spaces. Proponents argue that green spaces improve residents' mental health. However, a recent study found that residents of buildings with mandatory green spaces reported no greater life satisfaction than residents of buildings without them. Therefore, the requirement will do nothing to improve residents' mental health.
stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: assumes that life satisfaction is the only valid measure of mental health without establishing that other measures are irrelevant

choices.label: B
choices.text: fails to consider whether green spaces provide benefits unrelated to mental health, such as environmental sustainability

choices.label: C
choices.text: presupposes the conclusion it is attempting to establish by defining mental health in terms of life satisfaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: draws a general conclusion about all residents from a study that may have examined only a small and unrepresentative sample

choices.label: E
choices.text: overlooks the possibility that the city council had motivations other than improving mental health when passing the requirement

stimulus: A recent study found that cities with more coffee shops per capita have higher average standardized test scores among their residents. The study's authors concluded that drinking coffee improves cognitive performance, thereby boosting test scores.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the authors' conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some cities with very few coffee shops also have high standardized test scores.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coffee contains caffeine, which has been shown in laboratory settings to temporarily increase alertness in some individuals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cities with higher average incomes tend to have both more coffee shops and better-funded schools with superior test preparation resources.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The standardized tests measured in the study assessed a wide range of cognitive skills, including memory, reasoning, and reading comprehension.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coffee consumption has increased nationally over the same period during which standardized test scores have risen.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long recommended that people consume at least five servings of fruits and vegetables per day. A recent study, however, found that participants who consumed exactly five servings per day showed no significant improvement in cardiovascular health compared to participants who consumed fewer than five servings per day. The nutritionists' recommendation must therefore be based on something other than cardiovascular health benefits.
stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: fails to consider that the study's participants may not have accurately reported their daily fruit and vegetable consumption

choices.label: B
choices.text: presupposes that cardiovascular health is the only health benefit relevant to evaluating the nutritionists' recommendation

choices.label: C
choices.text: draws a conclusion about all nutritionists based on the findings of a single study

choices.label: D
choices.text: does not specify what quantity of fruits and vegetables would be sufficient to produce cardiovascular health benefits

choices.label: E
choices.text: overlooks the possibility that the participants who consumed fewer than five servings were already in excellent cardiovascular health

stimulus: A wildlife conservation group claims that the recent decline in the monarch butterfly population is caused entirely by the destruction of milkweed habitats along the butterflies' migration route. However, studies show that milkweed availability along the migration route has actually increased over the past decade, due to successful replanting initiatives. Therefore, milkweed destruction cannot be the primary cause of the monarch butterfly population decline.
stem: The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: assumes without justification that replanting initiatives will continue to be successful in future decades

choices.label: B
choices.text: fails to consider that the increased milkweed availability may itself be insufficient to reverse the effects of earlier habitat destruction that preceded the replanting initiatives

choices.label: C
choices.text: presupposes the truth of the conservation group's claim in order to argue against it

choices.label: D
choices.text: overlooks the possibility that the studies measuring milkweed availability may have been funded by parties with an interest in their outcome

choices.label: E
choices.text: takes for granted that correlation between two trends is sufficient to establish a causal relationship between them

stimulus: A city's parks department recently planted drought-resistant grass in Riverside Park to reduce water consumption. The department claims this initiative was a success because water usage at Riverside Park dropped by 30 percent in the year following the installation. However, that same year, the city experienced its wettest summer on record, with rainfall levels nearly triple the historical average.
stem: The argument above proceeds by doing which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Providing an alternative explanation that undermines a causal claim

choices.label: B
choices.text: Establishing that the parks department's conservation goals are ultimately unachievable

choices.label: C
choices.text: Demonstrating that drought-resistant grass is ineffective under normal weather conditions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conceding that the water reduction was significant while arguing it was insufficient

choices.label: E
choices.text: Questioning the accuracy of the measurements used to track water consumption

stimulus: A university's policy states that any student who misses more than three lectures in a course will be automatically removed from that course's enrollment. Theodora has not been removed from her enrollment in Philosophy 201. Therefore, Theodora has not missed more than three lectures in Philosophy 201.
stem: The reasoning in the argument above is most similar to which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any bridge built before 1940 requires immediate structural inspection. The Halverson Bridge requires immediate structural inspection. Therefore, the Halverson Bridge was built before 1940.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No employee who completes the safety training will be assigned to the overnight shift. Marcus has not completed the safety training. Therefore, Marcus will be assigned to the overnight shift.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any town with a population exceeding 50,000 is classified as a city. Millford is not classified as a city. Therefore, Millford's population does not exceed 50,000.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every applicant who submits a portfolio before the deadline will receive an interview. Diane received an interview. Therefore, Diane submitted her portfolio before the deadline.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If a patient is prescribed medication X, then that patient must avoid grapefruit. Dr. Chen's patient has been told to avoid grapefruit. Therefore, Dr. Chen's patient has been prescribed medication X.

stimulus: The city council argues that installing security cameras in Morrow Park will reduce crime there. They point to Dalton Square, where camera installation last year coincided with a 40 percent drop in reported crimes. However, this reasoning is flawed. Dalton Square underwent a major renovation at the same time the cameras were installed, transforming it into a popular destination with increased foot traffic throughout the day. Areas with higher foot traffic consistently show lower rates of certain crimes because potential offenders are deterred by the presence of witnesses.
stem: The argument above proceeds by doing which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Providing an alternative explanation for evidence cited in support of a conclusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Demonstrating that the city council's proposal would produce unintended consequences

choices.label: C
choices.text: Questioning the reliability of the crime statistics collected at Dalton Square

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arguing that security cameras are ineffective under any circumstances

choices.label: E
choices.text: Establishing that Morrow Park and Dalton Square are too dissimilar to be compared

stimulus: Nutritionists have long advised people to reduce their sodium intake in order to lower blood pressure. However, a recent large-scale study found that participants who consumed moderate amounts of sodium had lower rates of cardiovascular disease than those who consumed either very high or very low amounts. Some researchers have concluded from this study that the current dietary guidelines recommending low sodium intake are misguided.
stem: The researchers' conclusion is questionable because it fails to consider that which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the researchers' conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Many participants in the study who consumed very low amounts of sodium did so because they were already being treated for cardiovascular disease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium consumption varies significantly across different cultural and demographic groups worldwide.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some nutritionists had previously acknowledged that extremely low sodium intake might carry certain health risks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The study was funded in part by a trade organization representing the processed food industry.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blood pressure is only one of several factors that contribute to cardiovascular disease.

stimulus:
The city council argues that building a new downtown parking garage will reduce traffic congestion. Their reasoning is that drivers currently circle the streets searching for available parking spaces, and this circling behavior contributes significantly to congestion. If drivers could find parking quickly, they would spend less time on the road.

However, studies of cities that have built additional parking infrastructure show that total vehicle traffic in those areas increased substantially within two years of the garage openings. Apparently, the availability of convenient parking encourages more people to drive downtown who otherwise would have taken public transit.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument against the city council's position?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The proposed parking garage would be built on a lot that currently serves as a bus depot.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of people circling for parking downtown has increased by 15 percent over the last decade.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In cities where new parking garages were built, the increase in total vehicle traffic more than offset any reduction in circling behavior.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Public transit ridership tends to decline whenever gas prices fall below a certain threshold.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The city council has not commissioned an independent study on the environmental impact of the proposed garage.

stimulus: The city council argues that building a new downtown parking garage will reduce traffic congestion because more drivers will park in one central location rather than circling the streets looking for spaces. However, studies of cities that have built similar garages show that within two years of a garage's opening, total vehicle traffic in the surrounding area actually increases. Clearly, the new garage will not reduce congestion in Millbrook.
stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The city council has not considered any alternatives to building a parking garage for reducing traffic congestion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The increase in vehicle traffic observed in other cities was not offset by other congestion-reduction measures those cities implemented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: What has occurred in other cities after the construction of parking garages will also occur in Millbrook.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drivers in Millbrook currently spend a significant amount of time circling downtown streets looking for parking spaces.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Building a parking garage is the most expensive method the city council could choose to address traffic congestion.

stimulus: The city council recently passed an ordinance requiring all downtown restaurants to post their health inspection scores in their front windows. The council argued that this policy would improve public health by allowing diners to make informed choices, thereby pressuring low-scoring restaurants to improve their sanitation practices. However, a downtown restaurant association has pointed out that the three restaurants with the lowest inspection scores are all located in a block that is under heavy construction, making their storefronts completely inaccessible to pedestrians.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the city council's argument that the ordinance will pressure low-scoring restaurants to improve their sanitation practices?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Several high-scoring restaurants have voluntarily posted their inspection scores in their windows for years without being required to do so.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diners who cannot physically access a restaurant's storefront are unlikely to patronize that restaurant regardless of its inspection score.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The construction on the affected block is scheduled to last eighteen months, during which time none of the three low-scoring restaurants will lose customers due to posted scores.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some diners report that they rarely notice health inspection postings even when they are prominently displayed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The city council did not consult with restaurant owners before drafting the ordinance.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long advised people to reduce their intake of saturated fat in order to lower their risk of heart disease. However, a recent large-scale study found that participants who replaced saturated fat in their diets with refined carbohydrates showed no reduction in heart disease risk compared to those who made no dietary changes. The nutritionists' advice, therefore, must be flawed.
stem: The argument's reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: fails to consider that the participants in the study may not have accurately reported their dietary habits

choices.label: B
choices.text: presupposes that reducing saturated fat intake is the only effective method of lowering heart disease risk

choices.label: C
choices.text: draws a conclusion about the soundness of dietary advice based on evidence that concerns only one particular way of following that advice

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on a study whose sample size may be too small to yield statistically significant conclusions

choices.label: E
choices.text: assumes that heart disease risk is determined solely by dietary factors rather than by a combination of genetic and environmental influences

stimulus: The city council recently passed an ordinance requiring all downtown restaurants to post calorie counts on their menus. The council argued that this measure would reduce obesity rates by enabling customers to make more informed dietary choices. However, a recent study found that in cities with similar ordinances, average daily caloric intake among restaurant patrons did not decrease in the two years following implementation. Therefore, the ordinance will not reduce obesity rates in our city.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The study examined cities whose populations were, on average, younger and more physically active than the population of our city.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many downtown restaurants already displayed some nutritional information voluntarily before the ordinance was passed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obesity is influenced by factors beyond caloric intake, such as sleep patterns and genetic predisposition, and the ordinance has measurably improved those factors in comparable cities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: In cities with similar ordinances, residents who did not frequently dine at restaurants showed no change in obesity rates.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The council considered but ultimately rejected a separate proposal that would have imposed a tax on high-calorie menu items.

stimulus: All of the investigative journalists at the Herald have won at least one regional press award. Since Marcus has never won a regional press award, he cannot be an investigative journalist at the Herald. However, Marcus is clearly a skilled reporter — his articles consistently appear on the front page, and front-page placement at the Herald is reserved exclusively for the paper's most reliable staff members.
stem: The argument's conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every skilled reporter eventually wins a regional press award.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Marcus works at the Herald in some capacity other than as an investigative journalist.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The most reliable staff members at the Herald are not necessarily investigative journalists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Front-page placement is the most meaningful indicator of journalistic skill at any newspaper.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some investigative journalists at the Herald have never had articles appear on the front page.

stimulus: The city council argues that building a new stadium downtown will boost the local economy because stadiums attract visitors who spend money at nearby restaurants and hotels. However, economists have found that most people who attend stadium events are local residents who would have spent that same money elsewhere in the city if the stadium did not exist. Therefore, stadiums do not generate new economic activity — they merely redirect it.
stem: The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Local residents spend more money at restaurants and hotels than out-of-town visitors do.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The city council has not consulted with economists before proposing the stadium project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Money spent by local residents at the stadium does not constitute a net increase in economic activity for the city as a whole.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Building a new stadium would cause some existing local businesses to lose customers permanently.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Out-of-town visitors who attend stadium events would not visit the city for any other reason.

stimulus: The city council is considering whether to approve a new downtown parking garage. Supporters argue that the garage will reduce traffic congestion by giving drivers a clear destination rather than circling blocks searching for street parking. However, urban planners have consistently found that adding parking capacity in dense urban areas ultimately increases vehicle traffic, as greater parking availability encourages more people to drive downtown who otherwise would have taken public transit. Therefore, approving the garage would worsen, not improve, the very problem it is meant to solve.
stem: The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The city council has previously rejected proposals to expand public transit infrastructure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A sufficient number of people who currently take public transit downtown would instead drive if the garage were built.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Circling for street parking is the primary cause of traffic congestion in the downtown area.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urban planners are more qualified than city council members to make decisions about downtown development.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The parking garage would be profitable enough to offset the costs of increased traffic management.

stimulus: Nutritionists have long advised people to reduce their consumption of saturated fat in order to lower their risk of heart disease. However, a recent decade-long study found that participants who replaced saturated fat in their diets with refined carbohydrates showed no reduction in heart disease risk compared to those who made no dietary changes at all. The nutritionists' advice, therefore, cannot be correct.
stem: The argument's reasoning is flawed because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: assumes that the conclusion of the study applies equally to all demographic groups without considering whether the study's participants were representative of the broader population

choices.label: B
choices.text: treats evidence that one particular method of reducing heart disease risk is ineffective as evidence that reducing heart disease risk through dietary changes is impossible

choices.label: C
choices.text: concludes that dietary advice is incorrect on the grounds that one specific way of following that advice failed to produce the intended result

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on a study conducted over an insufficient period of time to draw meaningful conclusions about long-term health outcomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: mistakes a correlation between dietary habits and heart disease risk for a relationship of direct causation
